title: Born in Jackson on Christmas Day
summary: McLaurin recalls being born in Jackson on Christmas Day 1939 to John and Connie McLaurin, domestics who finished high school, his father landing a prized job with Coca-Cola, and his own place as the first in his immediate family to reach college.

title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
summary: He describes living with his Tuskegee-educated schoolteacher grandmother, who passed him the books she had bought for his uncle and filled his head with her heroes, especially Booker T. Washington, the self-educated former slave who came to speak to presidents.

title: The Train-Station Restroom
summary: At the segregated Jackson train station a tall white man accused the small McLaurin of peeping at white women in the restroom while he stood waiting for his grandmother, a crowd gathering around the frightened boy.

title: This Is My Boy
summary: His light-skinned grandmother emerged from the restroom, declared the boy was hers, and led him out, leaving the white man stunned that the woman he took for white had claimed the child.

title: Learning to Stay Away
summary: On the way home his grandmother taught him the rules of segregation, to stay away from white people and never be seen acting friendly toward white women, lessons he absorbed in a self-contained Black world on Farish Street.

title: Never Answer the Phone
summary: Working at a white-owned store as a boy, McLaurin was told never to answer the phone or get near the white men who drank there, until the manager fell ill and a group of white men demanded he pick up a ringing phone and take an order.

title: The Note That Broke the Rules
topic: A Note to a White Woman
summary: When he handed the order he had written to the white woman in charge, a white man seized the note in fury that a Black boy had written it and passed it to a white woman, insisting he should never have held a pencil, and McLaurin ran home to a lesson from his mother about the Klan.

title: The Furniture Auction in Rankin County
summary: He recounts being hired with friends by a furniture-store owner to set out, sell, and deliver furniture at an auction over in Rankin County, work that put the near-seventeen-year-old in an old Army shirt left from his reserve training.

title: The Army Shirt and the Constable
summary: An armed constable confronted him over the stripe on his Army shirt, demanding to know whether he had been over white men and threatening him, after which the store owner sent him home and would not use him again for fear of trouble.

title: Signing Up for the Reserve
summary: He tells how, tagging along while friends tried to enlist, he was the one who passed the test, and how the chance to get out of Jackson drew him toward six months of active-duty training and a reserve unit despite his mother's wish that he stay in school.

title: His Father's Signature
summary: Needing a parent's signature his mother refused, McLaurin turned to his father, who had been kept out of the war by a deformed foot and resented it, and who signed the papers that let his son go.

title: Dumped From the Bunk at Fort Jackson
summary: At Fort Jackson in 1955 he met a newly desegregated army still in transition, getting dumped from his top bunk for an unmade bed and struggling to keep the thirty-inch step on marches behind the long-legged men up front.

title: A Letter to the Congressman
summary: Wanting out, he wrote his Mississippi congressman John Bell Williams, only to have the reply read aloud by a sergeant: a little Black boy from Mississippi ought to be proud to wear the uniform, and his exit was denied.

title: Seeing Beyond Jackson
summary: Back in Jackson he returned to high school with new eyes, the Army having shown him how unprepared he was for the world beyond his community and how much he had not been exposed to, just as the Freedom Riders were coming to town.

title: A Ride From Medgar Evers
summary: He recalls a tall handsome stranger giving him and his friends a ride to the Farish Street theater, learning days later from the newspaper that the man was the NAACP leader Medgar Evers, which drew him to seek out the organization.

title: The Rock-Throwing Nights
summary: He describes the basketball rivalries between Lanier and Jim Hill highs, and the night white teenagers threw rocks through the Black community, prompting the young men to load up to retaliate before the Freedom Riders arrived.

title: Bevel's Challenge at the Pool Hall
summary: As the young men gathered weapons for a fight, the stranger James Bevel hopped onto a pool table and asked whether any of them were brave enough not to fight each other but to buy a ticket on the white side of the bus station.

title: Buying a Ticket Instead of a Fight
summary: Several of his friends, James Jones and Ralph Floyd among them, took Bevel's challenge and the money, called off the gang fight, and were arrested buying tickets, though they had not understood they would go to jail.

title: No Fear, Only Distance
summary: McLaurin explains that he and his friends were not afraid of white people, only taught to stay away, and that the photographs of Emmett Till in Jet and Ebony left them angry enough to talk of going to the Delta to find the killers.

title: Learning the Whole Picture
summary: As his jailed friends got out, Bevel kept coming back, explaining CORE, SNCC, and the NAACP and the wider civil rights picture, and the young men began thinking about developing a movement of their own in Jackson.

title: Jesse Harris and the Parchman Strap
summary: He retells, in his friend Jesse Harris's own grim and almost comic telling, the Parchman penitentiary where Freedom Riders were sent, the sign that read they would break your spirit and your behind, and the strapping Jesse took for refusing to count off as a number.

title: Building the Jackson Movement
summary: With Medgar Evers, Diane Nash, and others drawn in, the group set about building a Jackson movement, McLaurin moving toward a high-school equivalency exam while helping the effort through the summer of 1961.

title: The State Fair
summary: He describes the white state fair that came to Jackson with the burlesque show and attractions the Black fair never had, and his determination to get in, by slipping in if need be, because he was never a fearful person.

title: Arrested for Wanting In
summary: Turned away three times from the white fair he was told was a state fair for everyone, McLaurin went back until the police jailed him, and in that cell a pattern came clear: every time he stepped outside the perimeter set for Black folk, he got cut off.

title: Medgar's Bail and the Edwards Workshop
summary: Medgar Evers posted his bond and told him about a workshop at Mount Beulah near Edwards on voter registration and civil rights, run by the white lawyer William Higgs and lawyers brought from the North, a surprise in a state with only three Black lawyers.

title: Hearing Martin Luther King
summary: He recalls the thrill of hearing Martin Luther King speak at the Masonic Temple, admiring the oratory he had only known from Medgar, and even the green double-breasted suit King wore that McLaurin coveted.

title: The Murders in the Free Press
summary: Helping Medgar distribute Hazel Brannon Smith's Mississippi Free Press, McLaurin read of Aaron Henry in Clarksdale and the murder of Reverend George W. Lee in Belzoni, names and places that began to map the violence for him.

title: The Map of the Delta
summary: When McLaurin asked how they could stop the lynchings, Medgar Evers spread a map of Mississippi, drew a circle around the Delta, and explained how registering its Black majority could send representatives to the legislature.

title: Lawrence Guyot's Pitch
summary: On the Jackson State campus the big red-complexioned Lawrence Guyot pressed a voter-registration application into his hands, promised the vote would free them, and told him a man named Bob Moses was coming to set up a Delta project.

title: Diane Nash and the Old Hands
summary: At the Freedom House he met Guyot and others and absorbed how Bevel and Amzie Moore had already opened Ruleville through citizenship training, and how outspoken Diane Nash stood up front debating among men.

title: A Little Gangster Wants Out of the Box
topic: Why the Vote Mattered to Him
summary: McLaurin reflects on his old self-image as a little gangster admiring movie tough guys who feared no police, and how the promise that the vote would let him meet a white man as an equal spoke to his hatred of always stepping aside.

title: Waiting for the Giant Bob Moses
summary: Expecting a Paul Bunyan of a man from all the talk, McLaurin watched a small, quiet figure step from the background and say his name was Bob Moses, and found himself caught by the weight of what the unassuming man said before they set off for the Delta.

title: Sam Block's Greenwood
summary: Arriving near midnight in Greenwood, he learned of Sam Block, who slept in junkyard cars while organizing, set up a SNCC office above Barnes's photography studio, and had just been run out of it by the White Citizens Council.

title: The Anonymous Thousands
summary: He passes on Amzie Moore's observation that the census could never count all the Black folk packed into Greenwood, Greenville, and Clarksdale, people who lived so close and so guarded that they were functionally anonymous.

title: Midnight Through Ruleville
summary: He describes creeping through Ruleville after midnight in a convertible with Bob Moses, certain at any moment the police would spring out, and the sweet relief of finally seeing the town's sign behind them.

title: Amzie Moore's Brick House
summary: Reaching Amzie Moore's brick house in Cleveland surprised the Jackson boy who had seen no middle-class Black homes, and Moore told them of his NAACP organizing in overalls on the plantations and signing up members while his choir sang.

title: Sunday at Mount Galilee
summary: Bob Moses woke them for church at Mount Galilee Baptist, where the local leadership that had requested SNCC organizers, Joe McDonald, Mary Tucker, and others, gathered to begin their voter-registration drive.

title: The Leadership of Sunflower County
summary: He lays out Sunflower County, seventy percent Black yet white in every office, where intimidation and the murder of Emmett Till had run the NAACP out, and the local people who wanted SNCC's help to register without fear.

title: Arrested on the First Canvass
summary: On his first walk through the Ruleville community, McLaurin and his fellow organizers were arrested by an armed constable and warned by the mayor to go back to New York, and McLaurin insisted on being driven back so the watching Black community would see they had survived.

title: The First Trip to the Courthouse
summary: Charged with leading two elderly women into the Indianola courthouse to register, McLaurin found them already out of the car and walking ahead while he hung back looking for the Klan, only for the registrar's office to be closed.

title: I Got to Grow Up
summary: Standing in the courthouse beneath the colored-water-fountain sign with the jailhouse bars visible across the street, McLaurin thought of George W. Lee and Emmett Till and told himself he had to grow up and stop being frightened if he was going to stay.

title: Why They Went
summary: He reflects that the elderly people who walked up to register, born under the plantation's power, did it out of a hope that their children would not have to live as they had, and out of a wanting to get out of the box.

title: Fannie Lou Hamer Raises Her Hand
summary: At a mass meeting Mary Tucker brought in plantation folk, and among the next group to volunteer to go register was a woman McLaurin did not yet know, Fannie Lou Hamer, who raised her hand to board the bus to Indianola.

title: The Bus That Was Too Yellow
summary: He recounts the August 31 bus of eighteen people to register in Indianola, the police arresting the driver for the bus being too yellow, and the truckloads of armed white men who trailed them home with threats and insults.

title: Off the Marlow Plantation
summary: He tells how the plantation owner ordered Fannie Lou Hamer to withdraw her registration or leave, and her answer that she had not gone down to register for him but for herself, words that left McLaurin marveling at her courage.

title: The Mayor Takes Joe's Gun
summary: Ten nights later night riders shot up the Ruleville community, into the McDonald, Sisson, and Tucker homes where Hamer was staying, and the next day the mayor went to Joe McDonald's house and took his gun for safekeeping.

title: The Second Amendment, Folded Down
summary: McLaurin showed Joe McDonald the Second Amendment in his son's history book and folded the page down, and McDonald walked into city hall, looked the white man in the face for the first time, and walked out with his gun, becoming the next mass-meeting speaker.

title: Ella Baker, and Deciding to Stay
summary: Living Ella Baker's teaching to lift up the least and then get out of their way, McLaurin found in Fannie Lou Hamer's refusal to back down, even after her home community was shot up, the reason he decided to stay in the Delta.

title: Lost in the Hills for Fannie Lou Hamer
summary: Assigned by Bob Moses to find Fannie Lou Hamer, now living with relatives in Tallahatchie County, the Jackson-bred McLaurin got hopelessly lost on gravel roads, stuck in a ditch and pulled out by a white farmer he told he was visiting kin.

title: Have a Seat, I'll Be Right With You
summary: Finding the hilltop plantation shack at last in the pouring rain, McLaurin told Fannie Lou Hamer that Bob Moses wanted her at Tougaloo, and she, not knowing him from Adam, simply told him to have a seat and packed her bag, talking of her life all the way.

title: The Road to Nashville
summary: Tougaloo turned out to be a rendezvous for a SNCC conference at Fisk in Nashville, and McLaurin loaded into cars with Hollis Watkins, the Ladner sisters, Curtis Hayes, Victoria Gray, and Fannie Lou Hamer, surviving a tire blowout and a run off the road.

title: A Voice Discovered at Fisk
summary: At the Nashville conference Fannie Lou Hamer was discovered, weaving song and the stories of Shadrach, Daniel, and Paul and Silas into a testimony that held a room full of SNCC leaders spellbound and launched her tour of Northeastern campuses.

title: Writing the Movement's Reports
summary: With Charlie Cobb gone, McLaurin took over the reports and found himself in the middle of a thriving project, writing up local people's needs and grievances, from a veteran's discharge to plantation evictions, and empowering folk to overcome their fear.

title: Against Freedom Summer
summary: McLaurin admits he opposed Freedom Summer at the Biloxi meeting, since few voters had actually registered, even as he recognized that the work had built local organizations and pushed Mr. Charlie back off people's lives.

title: The Volunteers and Tracy Sugarman
summary: He recalls the volunteers coming through Oxford training after a man was killed, and meeting the older artist Tracy Sugarman, whom he had earlier stayed with in Connecticut and now welcomed onto his project to draw the movement.
related_events: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner

title: Yellow Cars and Conspicuous Help
summary: McLaurin describes placing well-off Northern volunteers, a tax accountant, a congressman's son, in sharecropper homes without running water, his unease at their conspicuous canary-yellow cars, and his fear that they would dominate the people they came to serve.

title: The Surprise of Reciprocal Respect
summary: To his astonishment the volunteers and the local families built reciprocal respect, Tracy Sugarman shopping with the Williams family, freedom schools filling with elders aged fifty-six to seventy-seven, resources flowing into the homes, and McLaurin's view of white people changed.

title: Nonviolence as Technique
summary: He reflects that the vote was always his single objective, that nonviolence was a technique rather than a creed to keep the state from wiping the movement out, and that Fannie Lou Hamer, more than any minister, made him feel close to God.

title: Freedom Schools and Mock Elections
summary: Blocked by the reading-and-writing and constitution-interpretation tests, the movement set up its own mock elections and the freedom schools Charlie Cobb devised, supplementing the four-month plantation schooling the state had denied Black children.

title: Travis Shot, the Food Cut Off
summary: He recounts the shooting of SNCC's Jimmy Travis near Greenwood that drew workers from across the South, John Doar coming for the Justice Department, and the Leflore County board cutting off federal surplus food to starve out the registration drive, answered by relief from Belafonte, Poitier, and Gregory.

title: The Freedom Ballot, and Why Women Ran
summary: For the Freedom ballot they ran Aaron Henry for governor and Ed King for lieutenant governor, and chose women, Hamer, Victoria Gray, Annie Devine, for Congress because in those communities the women were the brave and active ones not dependent on white employers.

title: Qualifying Hamer for Congress
topic: The Day at the Secretary of State
summary: Told to take the sixth-grade-educated Fannie Lou Hamer to legally qualify for Congress, McLaurin marveled at her readiness, and they faced a roomful of white women gawking at the two who wanted to run, then the five-hundred-dollar fee that a sharecropper might not clear in a year.

title: Campaign Manager by Default
summary: As the office neared closing on the last day to qualify, with no campaign manager arriving, Fannie Lou Hamer told McLaurin to put his own name on the papers, saying he knew as much about being a campaign manager as she did about running for Congress.

title: The Greenville Courtroom
summary: McLaurin reflects that SNCC made people do what they never imagined, then recounts the segregated Greenville city courtroom where, with Black spectators kept standing outside, he took a seat in the empty white section and was arrested for inciting a riot.

title: Three Times to the Supreme Court
topic: A Case That Would Not Die
summary: He describes how his Greenville case, defended by R. Jess Brown, went to the United States Supreme Court three times, only to be remanded as the Warren Court gave way to Nixon's, the city of Greenville bent on putting his Black behind in jail.

title: Presenting Himself at the Jail
summary: With Stokely Carmichael having warned Greenville officials that SNCC would break him out, McLaurin surprised the waiting police by walking straight into the jail to present himself, then quietly arranged with the decent chief Burnley to serve a couple of nights and be released to go to college.

title: Redistricting MS-2
summary: He explains how the Second Congressional District, once Black-majority and held by Reconstruction's John R. Lynch, was gerrymandered diagonally to dilute the Black vote, and how he presented a redistricting plan while Henry Kirksey drew the maps that would put Black officials in office.

title: The Atlantic City Compromise
summary: McLaurin recounts the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's rejection of the two-seat compromise at Atlantic City, Lyndon Johnson's fear of losing the South, and how the Democratic rebuff drove the white South into a now-solid Republican column.

title: Isaiah Montgomery's Shadow
summary: Noting that in 1962 only Mound Bayou held Black elected officials, McLaurin tells of learning that Isaiah Montgomery, the last Black legislator in the 1890s, voted with the whites to take away the vote, a history he found hard to reconcile against Fannie Lou Hamer's stand.

title: A Promise in the Hospital
summary: He recounts visiting the dying Fannie Lou Hamer in the Mound Bayou hospital, where she made him promise she would not be buried back on a plantation, a promise he made believing she would outlive him, only to learn days later that she had passed.

title: The Funeral Home
summary: Depressed by the run-down funeral home preparing to bury Mrs. Hamer simply, McLaurin was approached by the undertaker Henry Espy, who offered a burial fit for a president and the assurance not to worry about the money, and the body was turned over to him.

title: A Place Not on a Plantation
summary: Determined to keep his promise that she not be buried on a plantation, McLaurin found that Mrs. Hamer's own Freedom Farm land in town could serve, and attorney Cleve McDowell cleared the legal hurdle of burying within the city limits.

title: The Funeral No One Could Afford
summary: With the funeral nearing ten thousand dollars, Owen Brooks took over the planning and the Delta Foundation through MACE paid the bill, and Mrs. Hamer was buried on her own land, McLaurin hoping someone would one day pick up the bare grave site and run with it.

title: The Headstone and the Statue
summary: He traces how Head Start workers first raised money for a headstone, how Pat Thompson and others slowly built up the site, and how a hundred-thousand-dollar effort joined by supporters in New Jersey finally erected a statue of Mrs. Hamer surveying the Delta.

title: The March Against Fear
summary: McLaurin recalls James Meredith's 1966 March Against Fear, shot from ambush soon after stepping off, continued by King, McKissick, and Carmichael, and how the Hamer v. Campbell suit led the movement to route the march through the Delta to encourage registration.

title: Securing Greenwood
summary: He describes scouting Greenwood for the marchers' campsite, being run off the Mississippi Valley State campus by state troopers, finding Stokely Carmichael jailed for raising a tent at a school, and the plan to fill a Black park in the heart of the community.

title: The Night Black Power Was Born
summary: With the park overflowing, a freed Stokely Carmichael took the stage and called for Black power, a phrase Willie Ricks had been voicing, and McLaurin, named one of Carmichael's Mississippi comrades, reflects that Black power only ever meant Black people having a say in their own destiny through the vote.
